query: Are mesenchymal stem cells classified as multipotent in current scientific literature?

retrieved_docs.title: Regenerative Therapy in Pain -- Introduction -- Mesenchymal Stem Cell
retrieved_docs.content: According to the International Society for Cellular Therapy, the criteria for cells to be considered mesenchymal stem cells include: Plastic adherence when maintained under standard culture Expression of CD73, CD 90, and CD 105 and not the expression of CD11b, CD14, CD19, CD34, CD45, or HLA-DR Ability to differentiate into osteoblasts, chondroblasts, and adipocytes in vitro [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck Movements -- Embryology -- The Role of Mesenchymal Stem Cells in the Development of the Skeletal System
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are multipotent in that they can form a variety of progenitor cells and then differentiate into cells belonging to the bone, cartilage, muscle, and fat cell lineages. Skeletal stem cells reside in multiple sites, including the perichondrium of fetal bone, the perichondrium of adult bone, and bone marrow. [7] MSCs are currently used to repair articular cartilage and intervertebral discs. [8] [9] Another critical characteristic of MSCs is that they can form new MSCs, thus maintaining the pool of stem cells belonging to the mesenchymal lineage. The current concept is that multiple types of stem and progenitor cells cooperate in forming, developing, and maintaining cells belonging to the osteogenic and other lineages associated with the vertebral column. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Stem cell treatments for spinal cord injuries can be categorized into 2 main types: supportive and regenerative therapies. Supportive stem cell therapy employs nonneural stem cells, such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) derived from bone marrow, umbilical cord, or adipose tissue. These cells are administered intravenously or intrathecally due to limited migration to the target area and differentiation into neural cells. MSCs release neurotrophic factors that can repair the injured area; however, their ability to replenish the nervous system is limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Umbilical Cord -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenchymal stem cells found within the Wharton’s jelly of the umbilical cord express c-kit and telomerase activity, consistent with the markers of stem cells. [9] [10] [11] These cells can be easily extracted after delivery and offer a source of stem cells with fewer ethical considerations than other sources. [7] [10] These cells have shown the ability to differentiate into neurons and glia when exposed to specific growth factors. [9] Additionally, these cells may play a role in treating autoimmune disease due to their ability to suppress the secretion of interferon-gamma and transform growth factor-beta1. [10] A more recent study completed on rats showed that transplantation of Wharton’s jelly into the site of traumatic brain injury could reduce the amount of brain damage by reducing brain edema and increasing expression of brain-derived neurotrophic factor. [7] Researchers continue to investigate Wharton’s jelly and mesenchymal stem cells for their potential therapeutic and technological roles. [10] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoprogenitor Cells -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) collected from adipose and bone marrow tissue hold therapeutic value for various bone disease treatments. Current studies demonstrate the benefit of bone grafts based on combinations of MSC, biomimetic scaffolds, and growth factor delivery, which showed an increased osteogenic regeneration rate with minimal side effects. The specific mechanisms of cellular signaling in bone remodeling are essential in understanding the incorporation of newer effective treatment methods for numerous bone diseases. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Enchondroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoprogenitor cells: pluripotent mesenchymal stem cells in bone surfaces that growth factors can stimulate to differentiate into osteoblasts Osteoblasts: initiate mineralization and produce, transport, and arrange matrix proteins. Osteoblast actions are regulated by parathyroid hormone, vitamin D, estrogen, growth factors, cytokines, leptin, and low-density lipoprotein-related protein 5. Osteoblasts may become osteocytes or surface-lining cells of bones. Osteocytes: the most numerous cells in bone. Osteocytes participate in key bodily functions like calcium and phosphate homeostasis and cyclic adenosine monophosphate activation. Osteoclast: hematopoietic progenitor cell-derived bone cells responsible for bone resorption Bone matrix proteins include type 1 collagen and noncollagenous osteoblast-derived proteins. Homeobox genes encode the skeletal morphogenesis regulators. Mesenchyme from the following regions gives rise to different parts of the skeleton:

retrieved_docs.title: Fracture Healing Overview -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The cartilaginous (soft) callus begins to undergo endochondral ossification, and a medullary callus further supports the bridging soft callus. RANK-L is expressed, stimulating further differentiation of chondroblasts, chondroclasts, osteoblasts, and osteoclasts. As a result, the cartilaginous callus is resorbed and begins to calcify. Subperiosteally, woven bone continues to be laid down. The newly formed blood vessels continue to proliferate, allowing further migration of mesenchymal stem cells. At the end of this phase, a hard, calcified callus of immature bone forms. Bone callus formation is dependent upon appropriate relative motion between fracture fragments. [28] [29] Bone Remodelling (Continues for months to years after clinical union)

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Novel cell lineage tracking technologies have led to recent revisions in the model of hematopoiesis. Classically, the model is as described above: HSCs divide into multipotent progenitor cells that can further divide into common myeloid progenitors or common lymphoid progenitors. Revised models demonstrate fewer branch points and instead show a steady differentiation of heterogenous HSC to the final cell lines. This leads to a to a more varied pool of HSC that are partially differentiated as opposed to groups of identical precursors. Traditionally these HSC are differentiated by cell surface markers which vary between HSC, progenitor cells, and mature cells. Cell surface markers between these partially differentiated HSC do not necessarily change despite a change in gene signaling, which gave rise to the classical branching model. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoblasts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoblasts derive from two embryonic populations of mesenchymal stromal cells (or mesenchymal stem cells, MSCs). MSCs originating from the neural ectoderm can directly differentiate into osteoprogenitor cells that will become osteoblasts and form bone through intramembranous ossification (i.e., squamous bones of the calvaria and clavicle). MSCs originating from the paraxial mesoderm differentiate into axial skeleton osteoblasts, while the MSCs of the lateral plate mesoderm form osteoblasts of the appendicular skeleton. The axial and appendicular skeleton develop by endochondral ossification, with these osteoblasts deriving from intermediate perichondral cells or hypertrophic chondrocytes. Both the indirect and direct processes converge at osteoprogenitor cells (or preosteoblasts). [8] Osteoblasts are induced from the osteoprogenitor cells by numerous signals. Understanding of the osteogenic lineage remains incomplete. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoblasts -- Pathophysiology -- Overview of Osseous Components
retrieved_docs.content: Colloquially divided into "red" bone marrow (Medulla ossium rubra): active myeloid tissue where hemopoietic stem cells produce red blood cells; and "yellow" bone marrow (Medulla ossium flava): hematopoietically inactive, mesenchymal stem cells (stroma) that accumulate lipids As individuals age, the red marrow becomes yellow as the fat concentration increases. Yellow marrow can revert to red under physiologic stress requiring hemopoiesis. [27] [5] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoprogenitor Cells -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoprogenitor cells, also known as osteogenic cells, are stem cells in the bone that play a prodigal role in bone repair and growth (see Image. Cells in the Bone). [1] These cells are the precursors to the more specialized bone cells (osteocytes and osteoblasts) and reside in the bone marrow. Osteoprogenitor cells originate from infant mesenchymal cells and turn into spindle cells at the surface of matured bones. In developing bones, they appear more frequently and activate multifunctional stages to remodel the bones. The body loses the ability to synthesize or utilize more osteoprogenitor cells with age. Dysfunction of osteoprogenitor cells may delay ossification and lead to a spectrum of diseases such as dwarfism and Kashin-Beck disease. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1739
retrieved_docs.content: Bone marrow transplant is the term that classifies the Arabic phrase زراعة نخاع العظم, indicating that both expressions refer to the same medical procedure of transplanting hematopoietic stem cells from donor bone marrow, thereby linking the English and Arabic terminology for this treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: The radius is identified as an allowed qualifier in several specialized domains, indicating that it can be the focus of detailed study or intervention in each area. In ultrastructural research, the radius is an allowed qualifier, meaning that subcellular imaging and analysis are applicable to this bone. Anatomical and histological investigations also list the radius as an allowed qualifier, reflecting its significance in the study of bone structure and tissue composition. Cytological assessment includes the radius as an allowed qualifier, allowing cellular‑level examination of bone cells. In the context of transplantation, the radius is an allowed qualifier, signifying that it may be considered for grafting or transplant procedures. Clinically, the radius is the direct_procedure_site_of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna, highlighting its central role in orthopedic care and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is classified under the broader category إجراء تدخلي, indicating that it is an interventional procedure. Within this group, the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is considered a child or sub‑procedure, representing a spinal cord marrow imaging technique that falls under the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic procedures umbrella.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 968
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” designates specific medical contexts in which the word “radius” may be used. In this framework, “cytologie” (cytology) is an allowed qualifier when applied to the radius, indicating that cytological examination of the radial bone is recognized. Likewise, the radius itself serves as an allowed qualifier for both “transplantace” (transplantation) and “zranění” (injury), meaning that procedures involving transplantation of the radius and assessments of radial injuries are acknowledged medical applications. This highlights that cytological analysis, transplantation, and injury evaluation are all legitimate contexts for referencing the radius within medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is categorized under the broader Arabic term إجراء تدخلي (interventional procedure) and encompasses several specific interventions, including نقل الدم (blood transfusion), المعالجة الفيزيائية (physical therapy), and صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية (spinal cord marrow imaging). The English term blood transfusion is used to classify نقل الدم, indicating that both labels refer to the same therapeutic modality. Blood transfusion (نقل الدم) is recognized as a potential source of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and risk management when it is employed. Physical Treatment Methods are linked to المعالجة الفيزيائية, reflecting their role within the spectrum of physical therapy techniques; the earlier association of Physical Treatment Methods as a child of نقل الدم is inconsistent with standard classification and is therefore considered erroneous. Together, these relationships illustrate how miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic procedures are organized within interventional care, the dual linguistic labeling of blood transfusion, its possible adverse effects, and the proper hierarchical placement of physical treatment modalities under physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier is linked to the anatomical term “radius” across several medical domains. In the context of growth and development (růst a vývoj), the radius is subject to specific permissible qualifiers that describe its developmental characteristics. Similarly, in cytology (cytologie), the radius can be qualified in ways that reflect cellular and microscopic observations. Additionally, in transplantation (transplantace), the radius is associated with particular qualifiers that pertain to grafting, donor‑recipient considerations, and surgical techniques. Together, these relationships indicate that the radius is a relevant structure in growth and development, cytological analysis, and transplant procedures, and that specific qualifiers are allowed to accurately describe its role and attributes in each of these clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1761
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is classified as a type of تسريب, which itself is a kind of نقل الدم (blood transfusion) and belongs to the broader category of الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (therapeutic procedures not classified elsewhere). In addition to its relationship with تسريب, INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is also linked to فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and to نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), indicating that it shares characteristics with both apheresis procedures and self‑donated blood transfusion. نقل الدم encompasses نقل الدم الذاتي as a specific child category, and the term blood transfusion is used to classify نقل الدم. Blood transfusion is associated with complications of surgical procedures or medical care, highlighting that transfusion practices can be a cause of such complications. Together, these relationships illustrate how various procedural categories—INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, تسريب, فصادة الكريات البيض, and نقل الدم الذاتي—are interconnected within the overarching framework of blood‑related therapeutic interventions and their potential impact on patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: The radius is identified as an allowed qualifier in several medical contexts. In the fields of anatomy and histology, the radius is recognized as an allowed qualifier, indicating its relevance for structural and microscopic study. Cytology also lists the radius as an allowed qualifier, reflecting its importance in cellular analysis. Additionally, the radius serves as an allowed qualifier for transplantation, highlighting its consideration in grafting or surgical replacement procedures, and for injury (zranění), emphasizing its role in trauma assessment and management. This consistent designation of the radius as an allowed qualifier across anatomy, histology, cytology, transplantation, and injury underscores its central significance in both diagnostic and therapeutic aspects of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2273
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is used as a permissible descriptor for the radius in multiple medical contexts. It qualifies the radius with respect to growth and development (růst a vývoj), indicating that developmental changes of the radius can be described using this qualifier. It also appears in cytology, where “Allowed qualifier” is applied to cytological examinations of the radius. In addition, the qualifier is used when the radius is involved in transplantation procedures, signifying that transplant‑related documentation may include this allowed descriptor. Finally, “Allowed qualifier” is employed in the description of radius injuries, linking the qualifier to trauma‑related assessments of the bone. Together, these relationships show that the radius can be referenced with an allowed qualifier across developmental, cellular, surgical, and injury‑related aspects of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad classification that encompasses a variety of diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, and within this category the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is listed as a subordinate (child) procedure, indicating that spinal‑region imaging is considered one of the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is positioned within both فصادة الكريات البيض (leukapheresis) and نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), indicating that injection and implantation techniques are considered relevant to white‑blood‑cell filtration procedures as well as to the practice of using a patient’s own blood for transfusion. نقل الدم (blood transfusion) is classified as “blood transfusion” and serves as the parent category for نقل الدم الذاتي, establishing autologous blood transfusion as a specific subtype of the broader transfusion process. Physical Treatment Methods are also linked as a child of نقل الدم, suggesting that certain physical therapeutic approaches are conceptually grouped under the umbrella of blood transfusion practices. Importantly, نقل الدم is recognized as a potential cause of complications of surgical procedures or medical care, underscoring the need for careful risk assessment and management when transfusion is employed. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical network in which injection/implantation procedures, leukapheresis, autologous transfusion, and related physical treatments are interconnected within the larger context of blood transfusion and its clinical implications.
